query: Are AI coding assistants like Cursor and claude code making junior developers worse at fundamentals in 2025-2026

report.title: Research Report: The Double-Edged Sword of AI Code Assistants
report.summary: AI coding assistants demonstrably accelerate novice developer work and help them interpret error messages, but this speed comes at a cost. The core debate centers on debugging, where some specific AI frameworks show massive skill improvements while the general trend points toward an increased risk of fundamental gaps. What remains unknown is the long-term price of this accelerated productivity—whether the tendency for beginners to blindly trust AI outputs creates a permanent ceiling on their future expertise.

sections.heading: The Unambiguous Productivity Boost
sections.content: For junior developers, the initial encounter with an AI coding assistant feels like a superpower. The data confirms that AI assistance speeds up novice work, reduces their perceived workload, and helps them interpret cryptic error messages. [VERIFIED] In structured environments, this boost does not seem to harm short-term learning. [VERIFIED] One study even reported that using ChatGPT improved students' general thinking skills. [VERIFIED] This combination of speed and support appears to be a clear win, but it masks a more complex reality lurking just beneath the surface.

sections.heading: The Great Debugging Debate
sections.content:
Nowhere is the conflict more apparent than in the crucial skill of debugging. The evidence fractures completely on this point, creating two starkly different pictures of the AI-assisted future. 

This is where sources fracture. One 2025 study (https://arxiv.org/html/2510.03884v1) argues that debugging skills were shown to improve by a staggering 57% with the use of a specific AI framework. [CONTESTED]
A broader review of the literature (https://arxiv.org/html/2510.10165v3) directly contradicts this, highlighting that difficulties in debugging are a consistent and primary risk associated with AI code assistants, leading to practices like "vibe coding." [CONTESTED]
The conflict is a scope_difference — the optimistic 57% gain came from a specific, structured tool, while the pessimistic view describes the general, unstructured use of AI assistants in the wild. This suggests the *how* of AI integration may be more important than the *if*, leaving a critical question about which outcome will become the norm.

sections.heading: The Slow Erosion of Fundamentals
sections.content: The speed offered by AI assistants may be a Faustian bargain. Multiple sources confirm that beginners can over-trust AI, which may lead to them losing sight of important foundational concepts. [VERIFIED] This isn't just a theoretical risk; one study observed that most users blindly followed the steps provided by a Large Language Model without any critical evaluation of the output's veracity. [VERIFIED] A single source reports a potential solution, suggesting an experimental tool called FuseFL generated explanations for its fixes that novice developers found as clear and helpful as those from human experts. [UNVERIFIED] But without wider corroboration, this remains a hopeful outlier against a troubling trend: developers are learning to follow instructions, not to reason from first principles.

open_questions: If the documented short-term learning gains only appear when AI use is highly structured, what does that imply for the millions of developers using these tools in chaotic, real-world projects?

The most critical unanswered question is whether the habit of 'blindly following' AI suggestions ossifies over time, creating a generation of developers who are fast with tools but incapable of building from scratch when those tools fail.

What nobody has measured yet is the 'break-even point'—the moment where the time an AI assistant saves on initial creation is completely lost to debugging the subtle, fundamental errors it introduced.
